coredata.dc:title: An ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence sensor for the detection of HULC based on Au@Ag/GQDs as a signal indicator
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jing-jing
Shang, Lei
Jia, Li-Ping
Ma, Rong-Na
Zhang, Wei
Jia, Wen-Li
Wang, Huai-Sheng
Xu, Ke-Hua
coredata.dc:description: Liver cancer is one of the factors that threaten human life. Highly up-regulated in liver cancer (HULC), a kind of long non-coding RNA (lncRNA), is regarded as a favorable noninvasive biomarker for the early diagnosis of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). So a strategy focused on improving the sensitivity of HULC detection is urgently desired. In this work, a highly sensitive electrochemiluminescent (ECL) sensor was constructed using Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles/graphene quantum dots (Au@Ag/GQDs) as a signal indicator to detect HULC. Compared with the individual gold or silver nanoparticles, Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles exhibited large specific surface area, better catalysis and excellent electronic transmission capacity due to the synergistic effect between Au and Ag. Thus, the ECL performance of GQDs could be greatly promoted by Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles. Under the optimal conditions, this proposed ECL sensor exhibited ultrahigh sensitivity for quantitative detection of HULC with a wide linear range from 1 fM to 5 nM and a low detection limit of 0.3 fM (S/N = 3). These results showed that the sensor was promising for the sensitive detection of lncRNA in clinical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Graphene quantum dots
Au@Ag
HULC

Calibration curve of the proposed sensor for HULC detection
Specificity, stability, and reproducibility of the proposed ECL sensor

Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM of (A) GQDs; (B) Au NPs; (C) Au@Ag nanostructures (insert is the magnified pictures of Au@Ag nanostructures and (D) Au@Ag/GQDs nanocomposite.
UV–vis spectra of GQDs, Au NPs and Au@Ag core-shell nanostructures.

(A) EIS of (a) GCE/Au, (b) GCE/Au/CP, (c) GCE/Au/CP/MCH, (d) GCE/Au/CP/MCH/TP and (e) GCE/Au/CP/MCH/TP/DP-Au@Ag/GQDs in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4 − containing 0.1 M KCl; (B) CV cures of GCE, GCE/Au@Ag/GQDs and GCE/Au@Ag/GQDs/DP obtained in [Fe(CN)6]3−/4 −]; (C) ECL intensity of the different modified electrodes: (a) GCE, (b) GCE/Au, (c) GCE/Au/CP, (d) GCE/Au/CP/MCH, (e) GCE/Au/CP/MCH/TP and (f) GCE/Au/CP/MCH/TP/DP-Au@Ag/GQDs in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8; (D) The ECL performance of Au/GQDs, Ag/GQDs and Au@Ag/GQDs nanocomposites in PBS solution (pH = 7.0) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8.
The optimization of experimental conditions: (A) incubation time of CP; (B) incubation concentration of CP; (C) pH values of PBS and (D) different volumes of DP added into Au@Ag/GQDs solution.

(A) ECL intensity of the proposed sensor incubated in different concentrations of HULC in PBS solution (pH = 7.0) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8 (0, 1 fM, 10 fM, 100 fM, 1 pM, 10 pM, 100 pM, 1 nM and 5 nM); (B) the calibration plot between ECL intensity and log c HULC..

(A) ECL intensity variations of the sensor toward deferent interfering substances (50 pM of miRNA-16, β-actin, gapdh, CEA, 10 pM of HULC and 10 pM of HULC in mixed solution); (B) stability of the proposed ECL sensor toward 1 fM TP based on continuous cyclic scanning for 9 cycles; (C) reproducibility in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8 of 10 pM TP.
ECL sensor mechanism of detection HULC based on Au@Ag/GQDs as a signal indicator.
Results of ECL detection of HULC in serum samples.

head.ce:title: An ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence sensor for the detection of HULC based on Au@Ag/GQDs as a signal indicator

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng, Shandong 252059, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Shandong Normal University, Jinan 250014, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Liver cancer is one of the factors that threaten human life. Highly up-regulated in liver cancer (HULC), a kind of long non-coding RNA (lncRNA), is regarded as a favorable noninvasive biomarker for the early diagnosis of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). So a strategy focused on improving the sensitivity of HULC detection is urgently desired. In this work, a highly sensitive electrochemiluminescent (ECL) sensor was constructed using Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles/graphene quantum dots (Au@Ag/GQDs) as a signal indicator to detect HULC. Compared with the individual gold or silver nanoparticles, Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles exhibited large specific surface area, better catalysis and excellent electronic transmission capacity due to the synergistic effect between Au and Ag. Thus, the ECL performance of GQDs could be greatly promoted by Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles. Under the optimal conditions, this proposed ECL sensor exhibited ultrahigh sensitivity for quantitative detection of HULC with a wide linear range from 1 fM to 5 nM and a low detection limit of 0.3 fM (S/N = 3). These results showed that the sensor was promising for the sensitive detection of lncRNA in clinical application.

ce:section.ce:para: Now, hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is one of the most common cancer in the world, causing a large number of people death every year [1]. Therefore, earlier diagnosis of HCC is of great significance for people. Clinical studies have demonstrated that highly up-regulated in liver cancer (HULC) with a long non-coding RNA (lncRNA) could be used as a non-invasive biomarker for early diagnosis of HCC. Although lncRNA could be detected by several traditional methods, such as real-time polymerase chain reaction (rt-PCR), northern blot, and Co-Immunoprecipitation (Co-IP), HULC was limited to be detected due to inherent disadvantages of these methods [2]. For example, the test results of rt-PCR are unstable and need to be repeated several times. Northern blot is not good to cell and body because of its radioactive probe. Co-IP is only used to detect the proteins or lncRNA that is bound by proteins, but HULC is not included. As a result, looking for a safe and efficient technique in the diagnosis of HULC during early stage of liver cancer is still a challenge.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) has received much attention in recent years, owing to its properties of low background, sensitivity, rapidity and control ability [3]. These advantages of ECL make it become a potential tool to detect various samples specifically and high sensitively. There are many luminescence species in ECL applications, such as metal semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [4], Ru(bpy)3 2+ [5], luminol [6] and their analogues [7]. However, these luminescence probes have some disadvantages to some extent. For instance, metal semiconductor QDs and Ru(bpy)3 2+ are usually poisonous to cell and environment, not conducive to the detection of biological molecules.

As one of the newly emerging fascinating graphene materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have drawn great public attention due to their extraordinary properties, such as excellent solubility, low toxicity, better biocompatibility and favorable ECL reversibility [8]. The technologies of preparing GQDs are getting more and more perfect, including bottom-up [9,10] and top-down [11,12] methods, which make their applications become more common in electrochemical sensing, optical sensing, imaging and photoelectron catalysis [13–15]. However, the applications of GQDs in ECL sensors have been hampered because their low ECL intensity and non-recycle in an aqueous system, which are hard to satisfy detecting demand [16]. Therefore, the researchers often combine GQDs with other nanomaterials, especially the metal nanocomposites, to increase their luminescence intensity and stability. For example, Li et al. loaded Au nanoparticles (NPs) on graphene to promote carbon dots emission for sensitive detection of carcinoembryonic antigen [17]. It was also found that the ECL signals of GQDs were enhanced by silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs)-based 3D DNA networks [18]. Yu's team put graphene quantum dots on the surface of villous Au nanocages [19] and porous PtPd nanochains [20] respectively, achieving signal amplification. By this taken, nanometals do have the ability to enhance the ECL performance of GQDs.

In recent years, more attentions have been paid to bimetallic NPs with various architectures because of their good properties, including electronic, sensing, optical and catalytic properties [21–23]. The study found that some unique surface and physiochemical characteristics were presented in bimetallic NPs because of the synergistic effects between two metallic electronic states, which were different from monometal [24]. Among these bimetallic nanoparticles and nanostructures, Au@Ag core-shell nanostructures have attracted wide interests because of their catalytic, plasmonic and sensor-based applications [25–27]. Lee's group [28] found that the sensitivity was nearly 10-fold higher for naked-eye detection of surface bound antigens using immuno-labeled Au@Ag core-shell NPs, compared to Au NPs of similar size. The spherical plasmonic NPs (PNPs) of Au@Ag were chosen to match the maximum of the probes instead of the previous rod-shaped PNPs for improving the sensitivity of H2S detection [29]. So far, to our knowledge, there is little literature on using Au@Ag core-shell to promote the ECL signals of GQDs.

In our work, Au@Ag core-shell nanostructures were synthesized and combined with the GQDs as the signal indicator. A strong ECL intensity was obtained along with K2S2O8 as co-reaction reagent, overcoming the weakness of low luminescence intensity and instability of GQDs as well as improving the sensitivity of the proposed sensor. So this can be regarded as an alternative way to effectively detect HULC.

Potassium peroxydisulfate (K2S2O8), HAuCl4·4H2O, mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH), tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris), sodium citrate, l-cysteine, silver nitrate, citric acid and ascorbic acid (AA) were purchased from Aladdin Industry Corporation. Phosphate buffered solutions (PBS, pH 7.0) consists of 0.1 M Na2HPO4 and 0.1 M KH2PO4. 0.1 M K2S2O8 aqueous solution was served as the ECL electrolyte. All chemical reagents were analytical grade. Millipore ultrapure water (resistivity ≥ 18.2 MΩ·cm) was used throughout the experiments. Human serum samples were obtained from Liaocheng People's Hospital. The tris-EDTA buffered solution was prepared using 10 mM Tris-HCl and 1 mM EDTA (TE buffer) to stock the lncRNA probes. The oligonucleotides were purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biological Engineering Technology & Services Co., Ltd. (China) and purified using high-performance liquid chromatography. Their base sequences were as follows:

Capture probe (CP): (5′-GTT CGG TCC TTC TCA-(CH2)6-SH-3′), Target probe (TP): (5′-TGA GAA GGA CCG AAC GTC TAA CCC CGG AAC GAC GAC AT-3′) and sulfhydryl-modified detection probe (DP): (5’-SH-(CH2)3-TGT TCC GGT GTT AGC-3′). miRNA-16: (5′-CTCAACTGGTGTCGTGGAGTCGGCAATTCAGTTGAGCGCCAATA-3′), β-actin (5′-TGACGTGGACATCCGCAAAG-3′), gapdh (5′-GATGGGATTTCCATTGATGACA-3′) and CEA were used as interfering substances.

The ECL emission and electrochemical measurements were simultaneously carried out on a Model RFL-1 ECL analyzer (Xi'an Remex Instrument Co., Ltd., China) with the voltage of the photomultiplier tube (PMT) set at 800 V and with the auxiliary equipment of CHI 760C (Shanghai CH Instruments Co., China) electrochemical work station at room temperature. All experiments were conducted using a conventional three-electrode system with a modified GCE (3 mm diameter) as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the auxiliary electrode as well as an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) were measured on a CHI 760C electrochemical workstation. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained from a JEM-2100 electron microscope working at 200 kV (JEOL Ltd., Japan). UV–Vis absorption spectra measurement was performed on Lambda 750 spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA).

The GQDs were synthesized according to the previous report with a slight modification [30]. In a brief, 25 mL solution containing 40 mg mL−1 citric acid was placed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 8 h. The brown solution obtained was transferred into a dialysis bag (MW = 3500) and then concentrated in a rotary evaporator to obtain the GQDs. The concentrated solution was preserved at 4 °C for further use.

Au@Ag core-shell nanostructures were prepared according to the reported method [31]. First, Au NPs were prepared referring to the classical method [32] and used as Au seeds. Next, 15 μL AA (0.1 M), 3 μL AgNO3 (0.1 M), and 15 μL NaOH (0.1 M) were mixed with 4 mL obtained Au NPs in a round bottom flask under slowly stirring at room temperature. At this point, the pH of the mixture was about 8.5. This reaction was kept for 30 min. Then, 60 μL AA (0.1 M), 15 μL AgNO3 (0.1 M), and 75 μL NaOH (0.1 M) were added again. This step was repeated several times. Then the resulting particles were purified by centrifuging at 10000 rpm for 5 min, redispersed in 4 mL water, and preserved at 4 °C for further use. At the same time, Ag NPs were prepared referring to the previous report [33] for comparison.

1 mL l-cysteine (1 mg mL−1) were added into 2 mL Au@Ag core-shell NPs under tirring and kept for 2 h. Next, 2 mL prepared GQDs were added into the above solution, continuing for about 3 h. Last, the mixture was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 5 min, washed several times and redispersed in 2 mL TE buffer solutions. Then 20 μL DP (100 μM) was added into above solutions, and shocked overnight slightly. The mixture was centrifuged and resuspended in 2 mL TE buffer solutions again. Au/GQDs and Ag/GQDs nanocomposites were synthesized using the same synthesis processes.

The mechanism of proposing sensor is described as Scheme 1 . First of all, the GCE electrode was polished with 0.3 and 0.05 μm Al2O3 powder, ultrasonically cleaned with water and ethanol successively, and dried with nitrogen before modification. Next, the clean GCE was immersed into electrolyte containing 1 mM HAuCl4 and 2 mM Na2SO4 to deposit Au NPs at −0.2 V for 200 s, and dried with a high-purity nitrogen air flow. Then, 5 μL CP (3 μM) was dropped on GCE/Au NPs for 3 h to obtain GCE/Au NPs/CP electrode. Subsequently, the unspecific sites of modified electrode were blocked with 3 μL MCH (2 mM) for 1 h. Then, 5 μL HULC solution with different concentrations and 5 μL DP-Au@Ag/GQDs were dropped onto the GCE/Au NPs/CP/MCH electrode successively and incubated for 2 h respectively. The modified electrode was washed with TE buffer before every step of modification. The obtained electrode was prepared for ECL detection in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8.

The structures and morphologies of GQDs, Au NPs, Au@Ag and Au@Ag/GQDs were characterized by TEM. It can be seen that the uniform spherical GQDs have an average size of about 3–5 nm with a homogeneous dispersion (Fig. 1A). Au NPs show uniform size with an average at 15 nm in Fig. 1B. As shown in Fig. 1C, the Au core and the Ag shell could be distinguished in the case of an outer bright and a central dark according to the different electronic density of Au and Ag atoms. After GQDs were loaded on the surface of Au@Ag NPs, a similar carbon membrane around Au@Ag nanostructures was formed (Fig. 1D). It can be concluded that it was a successful combination of GQDs and Au@Ag NPs, which could promote the electrochemical luminescence of GQDs effectively.

Then the optical performance of GQDs, Au NPs and Au@Ag core-shell nanostructures were investigated by UV–vis absorption. As shown in Fig. 2 , a strong typical absorption peak of GQDs typical absorption peak was observed at around 300 nm, due to π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains. The typical peaks of Au NPs were observed at 245 nm and 524 nm respectively. It was worth noting that the as-prepared Au@Ag core-shell NPs exhibited a strong absorption peak at around 400 nm. This maybe due to the reduced Ag on the gold surface resulted in a significant blue shift, which was consistent with previous literature report [31]. It further demonstrated that the Au@Ag core-shell NPs were successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on the electrochemical performance of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In order to characterize the processes of modifying the electrodes, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed in detection solution containing 0.1 M KCl and 2.5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4 −, which was recognized one of the most effective method to characterize the variation of the electrode surface [34]. As shown in Fig. 3A, the Ret of GCE/Au was small (Fig. 3A, curve a), owing to the good electrical conductivity of Au NPs. After the electrode was immobilized with CP (Fig. 3A, curve b) and then incubated with MCH (Fig. 3A, curve c), the Ret response continued to increase due to the poor conductivity of CP and the MCH film. Afterward, the target HULC (Fig. 3A, curve d) was hybridized with CP and then continued to work with DP-Au@Ag/GQDs (Fig. 3A, curve e), the Ret was further enhanced. To confirm whether the DP was successfully attached to the Au@Ag/GQDs, cyclic voltammetric behaviors of Au@Ag/GQDs and Au@Ag/GQDs/DP were examined. As shown in Fig. 3B, the CV of bare GCE contained a typical pair of reversible redox peaks, corresponding to the reversible redox reaction. When Au@Ag/GQDs were modified on the electrode, oxidation peak currents increased 27.42%. However, after Au@Ag/GQDs/DP was loaded on the electrode, the redox peak decreased 40.95% compared with Au@Ag/GQDs, because of the poor conductivity of DP. The CV results indicated that a considerable amount of DP were successfully loaded on the surface of Au@Ag/GQDs. These results demonstrated the sensor was fabricated successfully.

Next, the proposed ECL sensor was further investigated by recording ECL signals during different assembly stages. As shown in Fig. 3C, the ECL intensity of bare GCE was relatively low (Fig. 3C, curve a). This ECL signal was obtained as follows [35]: S2O8 2− was reduced to the strong oxidant SO4
−, meanwhile O2 was reduced to OOH. The OOH could react with SO4
− and produced light-emitting species 1(O2)2⁎, then light emitted. When the Au NPs were electrodeposited on the electrode, the ECL intensity was increased (Fig. 3C, curve b), because gold NPs owed good electrical conductivity and higher surface area, promoting the reduction of S2O8 2− and O2. After CP, MCH and target HULC were bonded onto the Au/GCE in succession, the ECL intensity decreased gradually (Fig. 3C, curve c–e), because they had weak electrical conductivity and occupied the active sites of Au NPs, thus depressing the reaction of S2O8 2− and O2 on the surface of electrode. It was worth noting that ECL intensity exhibited a sharp increase after the DP-Au@Ag/GQDs was immobilized on the electrode, because GQDs produced strong electrochemical luminescence promoted by Au@Ag core-shell NPs (Fig. 3C, curve f). The possible mechanisms for the ECL response of GQDs in the presence of S2O8 2− are described as follows [18]:
GQDs + ne → nGQDs ˙
−
S 2
O 8
2 −
+ e →
S 2
O 8
˙
2 −
S 2
O 8
˙
2 −
→
SO 4
2 −
+
SO 4
˙
−
GQDs ˙
−
+
SO 4
˙
−
→
GQDs ∗
+
SO 4
2 −
GQDs ∗
→ GQDs + hv

Firstly, S2O8 2− and GQDs were electrochemically reduced to generate strongly oxidizing SO4
− radicals and GQDs − radicals, respectively. Then, SO4
− radicals could react with GQDs − via electron-transfer annihilation, producing an excited state (GQDs*) that finally emitted light.

In order to identify the superior performance of Au@Ag, the ECL intensity Au@Ag/GQDs in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.1 M S2O8 2− was compared with those of Au/GQDs and Ag/GQDs (Fig. 3A). As can be seen, the Au/GQDs and Ag/GQDs produced obvious ECL signals, because Au NPs possessed the excellent conductivity and could accelerate the electron transfer on the one hand, and on the other hand the Ag NPs could promote the reduction of GQDs and improve the quantity of reduced GQDs (GQDs−) and excited GQDs (GQDs*), thus enhancing the ECL response of GQDs significantly [18]. It was worth noting that Au@Ag core-shell NPs had better performance than individual Au and Ag NPs on promoting the electrochemical luminescence of GQDs, because the electronic structure in the core-shell architecture could be modified through the interaction of the two kinds of atoms in relation to the so-called ligand and strain effects, which might endow them enhanced characteristics [36]. So, the Au@Ag NPs could exploit the excellent conductivity of Au NPs and the great electrochemical reactivity of Ag NPs, hence amplified the ECL responses. In addition, extensive studies have shown that the properties of the core-shell NPs strongly depend on their composition [37]. So we synthesized Au@Ag core-shell nanoparticles with different shell thickness (Fig. S1), and studied their influence on the electrochemical luminescence properties of GQDs. It turned out that the ECL intensity increased with the expanded shell thickness of Au@Ag (Fig. S2), but became unstable when the molar ratio of Au and Ag exceeded 1:1 (Fig. S3). For this reason, Au@Ag core-shell NPs synthesized by the molar ratio of 1:1 were applied to this experiment, which have enhanced and stable performance on promoting the emission of GQDs. Therefore, the Au@Ag/GQDs composites were chosen as a signal indicator to detect HULC sensitively.

In order to acquire an optimal ECL response, the effects of pH values, the concentration and incubation time of CP as well as the amount of DP added into Au@Ag/GQDs solution were investigated (Fig. 4 ). The ECL intensities of the developed sensor increased with the incubation time of CP in the beginning and then tended to reach a plateau (Fig. 4A). On the other hand, it increased first and then decreased as the CP concentration exceeded 3 μM (Fig. 4B). We suspected that when excess CP were loaded on the surface of the electrode, they were intertwined with each other, and could not be effectively combined with the TP, resulting decline in the ECL intensity. Herein, 3 μM concentration of CP and 3 h of incubation time were selected to immobilize the CP. The pH value of the detection solution and the amount of DP were also optimized, as shown in Fig. 4C and D. Both of their ECL intensity were increased first and then decreasing after reaching a maximum value. The strongest ECL intensities were obtained at pH 7.0 and 20 μL DP. Thus, the PBS at pH = 7.0 was selected as the detection solution and 20 μL DP as the optimal amount of addition in this experiment. Under the optional conditions, the designed sensor could exhibit an optimal ECL signal for the quantitative detection of HULC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration curve of the proposed sensor for HULC detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experimental conditions, an ultrasensitive ECL sensor was constructed using Au@Ag/GQDs complex as signal indicator for detection of HULC. The ECL intensities of different concentrations of HULC were shown in Fig. 5A. As can be seen in Fig. 5B, the ECL intensity had good linear relationship with the concentrations of HULC in the range from 1 fM to 5 nM with a low detection limit of 0.3 fM (S/N = 3). The liner relationship was represented as follows: I E = 5008.7 + 658.48 log c HULC (R = 0.9972). Compared with other existing methods (Table S1), the proposed ECL sensor possessed a lower detection limit and high sensitivity, attributing to the high electron transfer capability, large specific surface area and good biocompatibility of Au@Ag core-shell NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, stability, and reproducibility of the proposed ECL sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the proposed ECL sensor was investigated by using several potential interfering substances, such as miRNA-16, β-actin, gapdh, CEA, HULC and HULC mixture. When 5-fold excess of the interfering substances (50 pM miRNA-16, β-actin, gapdh, and CEA) were present, the ECL intensities were as low as the blank sample (Fig. 6A). However, when 10 pM HULC was added into the above interfering system, the ECL response of the mixture was close to 10 pM pure HULC target. These results indicated that the response was generated from the capture-probe-HULC target-specific interaction, and only the perfectly matched sequence could trigger dual-signal amplification strategies. So, the proposed ECL sensor exhibited excellent selectivity for HULC.

In order to evaluate the stability of the fabricate ECL sensor, the same electrode was detected by continuous 9 cycles under the optimal experiment conditions. As can be seen in Fig. 6B, the ECL intensity did not change significantly. Therefore, the developed ECL sensor had good stability.

To investigate the reproducibility of the proposed ECL sensor, we challenged the system with five different electrodes in the same batch, using the same concentration of HULC. The relative standard (RSD) was <5%, suggesting the reproducibility of the developed ECL sensor was acceptable (Fig. 6C).

The fabricated ECL sensor was applied to detect HULC in health human serum samples by a standard addition method. The serum was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min and then diluted 10-fold by TE buffer before using. When the serum samples were spiked with 10 pM, 50 pM, and 100 pM HULC respectively, the results were obtained and present in Table 1 . The RSD ranged from 5.6% to 8.2% and the recoveries were from 99.7% to 103.6%. Therefore, the developed ECL sensor could be applied in the quantitative determination of HULC in real samples.

In this work, an ultrasensitive sandwich-type ECL sensor was developed based on the electrodeposited Au NPs as the matrix and Au@Ag/GQDs as a signal indicator. The sensor displayed a wide detection range, extremely low detection limit, good selectivity, acceptable stability and reproducibility. It might provide wide potential application for the fast and economical quantitative detection of HULC in the early clinical diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21375055, 21505063, 21405070, 21427808), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017BB084, ZR2015BQ007, ZR2018BB059) and the Shandong Tai-Shan Scholar Research Fund.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2018.07.044.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jing-Jing Li is currently studying for the master's degree in the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaocheng University. Now her research interests focus on the development of biosensors.

Lei Shang received his Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Wuhan University in 2015. He is currently a lecturer at Liaocheng University. His current research fields focus on the development of novel material and mechanism for biosensors.

Li-Ping Jia received her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Soochow University in 2013. She is currently an associate professor at Liaocheng University. Her current research fields focus on the development of novel chem/biosensors.

Rong-Na Ma received her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Nanjing University in 2014, and she is currently a lecturer at Liaocheng University. Her research interests include electrochemical analysis, biosensors and biofunctional materials.

Wei Zhang received her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2016. She is currently a lecturer at Liaocheng University. Her current research fields focus on the development of novel electrochemiluminescent material for biosensors.

Wen-Li Jia received her master degree of science in Department of Chemistry from Liaocheng University, China, in 2006. She is a lecturer at Liaocheng University and a Ph.D. student in the College of Science at Xi'an Jiaotong University since 2016. Her main area of interest is electrochemistry.

Huai-Sheng Wang, professor of chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Liaocheng University. He received his Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Nanjing University in 2002. His scientific interests cover electrochemistry, biosensors and new materials.
